```{r}
# Load necessary libraries

library(ggplot2)                                                                # Used for creating visualizations like scatter plots, histograms, etc.
library(dplyr)                                                                  # Provides functions for data manipulation such as filter, select, mutate, and summarize
library(corrplot)                                                               # Useful for plotting correlation matrices to visualize relationships between variables
library(caret)                                                                  # For confusion matrix and performance metrics to evaluate how good the prediction model is 
library(pROC)                                                                   # To draw an ROC curve, and to Calculate AUC (Area Under Curve) for evaluating model performance
library(rpart)                                                                  # Load rpart for building decision tree models
library(rpart.plot)                                                             # Load rpart.plot to visually plot and interpret the tree structure
library(randomForest)                                                           # Used to build Random Forest models, for classification and regression
```
```{r}
# Load data set
df <- read.csv("C:\\Online_Shopper_Intention_data.csv")
# 'df' is now our working data frame containing the online shoppers' data

# Explore the structure of the data set
str(df)                                                                         # Gives an overview of the data types and variable structure
summary(df)                                                                     # Provides summary statistics (mean, median, min, max, etc.) for each variable
```
```{r}
# Convert relevant categorical variables columns to factors
df$Month <- as.factor(df$Month)                                                 # Months like "Feb", "Mar", etc. are categories, not text
df$VisitorType <- as.factor(df$VisitorType)                                     # Categories like "Returning_Visitor", "New_Visitor"
df$Weekend <- as.factor(df$Weekend)                                             # TRUE or FALSE – should be treated as a category
df$Revenue <- as.factor(df$Revenue)                                             # Target variable: whether the session led to a purchase
```
```{r}
# Create a correlation heatmap for numeric variables
numeric_df <- df %>% select(where(is.numeric))                                  # First, select only the numeric columns from the dataset
corr_matrix <- cor(numeric_df)                                                  # Calculate the correlation matrix
corrplot(corr_matrix, method = "color", type = "upper", tl.cex = 0.7)           # Plot the upper triangle of the correlation matrix
```
```{r}
# Hypothesis 1: Testing if PageValues differ based on Revenue outcome

ggplot(df, aes(x = Revenue, y = PageValues, fill = Revenue)) +                  # Create a boxplot to visually compare PageValues for Revenue = TRUE vs FALSE
  geom_boxplot() +                                                              # To reveal if high PgaeValues laed to more purchases 
  ggtitle("PageValues vs Revenue")

cor.test(df$PageValues, as.numeric(as.factor(df$Revenue)))                      # Test for correlation between PageValues and Revenue (converted to numeric)
# Note: We are using as.numeric(as.factor(df$Revenue)) to convert the logical factor (TRUE/FALSE) to numeric form, so that a Pearson correlation can be computed
```
```{r}
# Hypothesis 2: Testing if Revenue is Influenced by SpecialDay

ggplot(df, aes(x = SpecialDay, fill = Revenue)) +                               # Histogram to compare SpecialDay values across Revenue outcomes 
  geom_histogram(position = "dodge", bins = 10, alpha = 0.7) +                  # To reveal if users closer to special days are more likely to make a purchase
  ggtitle("SpecialDay vs Revenue")

t.test(SpecialDay ~ Revenue, data = df)                                         # T-test to compare average SpecialDay values for purchases vs no purchases
```
```{r}
# Hypothesis 3: Testing if Revenue is Dependent on Traffic Type

ggplot(df, aes(x = TrafficType, fill = Revenue)) +                              # Stacked bar chart showing proportion of revenue outcomes across different traffic types
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +                                                 # To reveal different traffic type causing purchases
  ggtitle("Traffic Type vs Revenue") +
  ylab("Proportion")

chisq.test(table(df$TrafficType, df$Revenue))                                   # Running a Chi-square test to check if Revenue and TrafficType are dependent

test <- chisq.test(table(df$TrafficType, df$Revenue),                           # Alternative version with simulated p-value for accuracy in case of small cell counts
        simulate.p.value = TRUE, B=2000)  
print(test)
```
```{r}
# Logistic Regression Model to Predict Revenue

df$Revenue_binary <- ifelse(df$Revenue == TRUE, 1, 0)                           # Convert Revenue to binary numeric (1 = TRUE, 0 = FALSE)

# Splitting the data into training and test sets
set.seed(123)                                                                   # for reproducibility
train_index <- createDataPartition(df$Revenue_binary, p = 0.7, list = FALSE)
train_data <- df[train_index, ]
test_data <- df[-train_index, ]

# Fit the logistic regression model
model_logit <- glm(Revenue_binary ~ PageValues + SpecialDay + TrafficType,      # We'll use some selected predictors to compare prediction models
                   data = train_data, 
                   family = "binomial")

summary(model_logit)                                                            # Summary of the logistic regression model

pred_probs <- predict(model_logit, newdata = test_data, type = "response")      # Predict probabilities and classes
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_probs > 0.5, 1, 0)

conf_matrix <- confusionMatrix(as.factor(pred_class),                           # Evaluate the model for accuracy and p-value
                               as.factor(test_data$Revenue_binary))
print(conf_matrix)

roc_obj <- roc(test_data$Revenue_binary, pred_probs)                            # ROC Curve and AUC
plot(roc_obj, main = "ROC Curve - Logistic Regression")
auc(roc_obj)
```
```{r}
# Train the decision tree model on training data

tree_model <- rpart(Revenue_binary ~ PageValues + SpecialDay + TrafficType,     # Using the same variables to compare
                    data = train_data, method = "class")

# Plot the decision tree
rpart.plot(tree_model, extra = 106)                                             

tree_predictions <- predict(tree_model, test_data, type = "class")              # Make predictions on the test set

tree_predictions <- factor(tree_predictions, levels = c(0, 1))                  # Ensure predicted and actual values are factors with same levels
test_data$Revenue_binary <- factor(test_data$Revenue_binary, levels = c(0, 1)) 

length(tree_predictions) == length(test_data$Revenue_binary)                    # Confirm lengths match for confusion matrix

confusionMatrix(tree_predictions, test_data$Revenue_binary)                     # Compute confusion matrix to evaluate the model for accuracy and others
```
```{r}
# Random Forest Model for Predicting Revenue Generation

train_data$Revenue_binary <- factor(train_data$Revenue_binary, levels = c(0, 1))# Ensure the target variable is a factor with levels 0 and 1 for classification
test_data$Revenue_binary <- factor(test_data$Revenue_binary, levels = c(0, 1))

# Build the Random Forest classification model using the same selected predictors 
rf_model <- randomForest(Revenue_binary ~ PageValues + SpecialDay + TrafficType,       
                         data = train_data,
                         importance = TRUE)

rf_predictions <- predict(rf_model, test_data)                                  # Predict classes (0 or 1) on the test data

rf_predictions <- factor(rf_predictions, levels = c(0, 1))                      # Convert predictions to factor with same levels as test data to avoid errors in confusion matrix
test_actuals <- factor(test_data$Revenue_binary, levels = c(0, 1))

confusionMatrix(rf_predictions, test_actuals)                                   # Evaluate the model using confusion matrix and classification metrics
```
```{r}
# Using Feature Importance to rank variables in terms of contribution

features <- c("Administrative", "Administrative_Duration", "BounceRates",       # Define predictors and target
              "ExitRates", "ProductRelated", "ProductRelated_Duration", 
              "PageValues", "SpecialDay", "TrafficType")
train_data_subset <- train_data[, c(features, "Revenue_binary")]

# Train Random Forest
rf_model <- randomForest(Revenue_binary ~ ., data = train_data_subset,          
                         importance = TRUE)

importance(rf_model)                                                            # View variable importance

varImpPlot(rf_model, main = "Feature Importance from Random Forest")            # Plot variable importance
```
```{r}
# Linear Regression Model to Predict Page Engagement
# To show that my data is binary and a linear regression model is not the best fit for such a dataset

set.seed(123)                                                                   # Set seed for reproducibility

engagement_data <- df[, c("PageValues", "ExitRates", "BounceRates",             # Select relevant predictors for engagement
                          "ProductRelated_Duration")] 
# Note: These are behavioral features that could affect perceived value

engagement_data <- na.omit(engagement_data)                                     # Remove any rows with NA values (just in case)

train_index <- createDataPartition(engagement_data$PageValues, p = 0.8,         # Split into train and test sets
                                   list = FALSE)
train_data <- engagement_data[train_index, ]
test_data <- engagement_data[-train_index, ]

# Fit the linear regression model
lm_model <- lm(PageValues ~ ExitRates + BounceRates + ProductRelated_Duration, data = train_data)

summary(lm_model)                                                               # Print summary of the model

lm_predictions <- predict(lm_model, newdata = test_data)                        # Predict on test data

lm_rmse <- RMSE(lm_predictions, test_data$PageValues)                           # Evaluate model performance
lm_r2 <- R2(lm_predictions, test_data$PageValues)

cat("\nModel Performance (Linear Regression):")                                 # Output performance metrics
cat("\nRMSE:", round(lm_rmse, 4))
cat("\nR-squared:", round(lm_r2, 4))
```
